serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Vietnamese chicken fried rice weighs approximately 450 g, representing a typical single-serve restaurant or hawker portion.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Rice, fried, with meat (SR Legacy ID 35188)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) — Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact Vietnamese chicken fried rice entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA tables as a standalone dish with a full nutrient panel. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Rice, fried, with meat' (ID 35188) is the closest approved-source equivalent and was used as the primary nutrient anchor. Input per_100g values from the GrabFood-sourced record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against this USDA SR Legacy entry and retained where consistent. Energy 151 kcal/100g, protein 7.8 g, fat 5.6 g, carbohydrate 17.8 g are within the expected range for chicken fried rice per USDA SR Legacy data. Sodium 333 mg/100g is plausible given soy sauce and fish sauce seasoning typical of Vietnamese preparation. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, vitamin C, cholesterol, saturated fat, trans fat) are retained from input and are consistent with USDA SR Legacy composite ranges. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, water_g, and most vitamins set to -1 as detailed breakdown not available in the approved source for this composite dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database — Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese chicken fried rice in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 64 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for fried rice (white rice base, stir-fried) as published in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC, 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008', Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, which is a peer-reviewed journal source. This is the closest approved analogous food. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (64 × 80.0 g) / 100 = 51.2, where available carbohydrate per serving = 17.78 g/100g × 450 g serving = 80.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 333 mg per 100 g from soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1,500 mg sodium, representing 65% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced condiments (soy sauce, fish sauce, oyster sauce) and opt for low-sodium soy sauce alternatives to keep a single serving below 600 mg sodium.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: White rice base with GI ~64 and a glycemic load of 51.2 per 450 g serving; dietary fibre is low at 0.89 g/100g, providing minimal attenuation of postprandial glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to 150–200 g, substitute white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower glycemic load, and increase the proportion of vegetables and lean chicken relative to rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Cholesterol 55.56 mg/100g and saturated fat 1.56 g/100g from egg and chicken; sodium 333 mg/100g adds cardiovascular risk through blood pressure elevation; trans fat is low at 0.02 g/100g.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals managing cardiovascular disease should limit portion to 200–250 g, request cooking with minimal oil, reduce sodium-containing condiments, and choose skinless chicken breast to lower saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High-heat stir-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that may exacerbate joint inflammation; sodium 333 mg/100g can worsen RA-related tissue oedema.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should request lower-heat cooking where possible, reduce sodium-containing condiments, and consider adding anti-inflammatory ingredients such as ginger or turmeric.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chicken fried rice (Vietnamese: Cơm chiên gà) is a Vietnamese-style stir-fried rice dish prepared in a wok over high heat. Cooked white rice is tossed with diced or shredded chicken (typically breast or thigh), beaten egg, and vegetables such as peas, carrots, and spring onion, seasoned with soy sauce, fish sauce, and oyster sauce. The high-heat wok technique produces lightly charred, separated rice grains with a savoury umami flavour and a slightly smoky aroma. The texture is firm and dry rather than sticky. Per 100 g the dish provides approximately 151 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 5.6 g fat, and 17.8 g carbohydrate with only 0.89 g dietary fibre, making it a moderate-energy, protein-containing staple. Sodium is elevated at 333 mg/100g due to multiple condiments. A standard restaurant plate weighs approximately 450 g, delivering around 680 kcal, 35 g protein, and 1,500 mg sodium per serving. Clinically, the dish is unsuitable for hypertension without sodium reduction, poses a high glycemic load for Type 2 Diabetes management, and carries moderate cardiovascular risk from cholesterol and sodium. It is commonly served as a main meal in Vietnamese restaurants and hawker stalls across Southeast Asia and Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chicken fried rice?
Can diabetics eat chicken fried rice?
Is chicken fried rice high in sodium?
Is chicken fried rice suitable for hypertension?

confidence_reason: No exact Vietnamese chicken fried rice entry was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as a standalone dish with a full nutrient panel. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy 'Rice, fried, with meat' (ID 35188) and cross-checked against the input ai_generated values (confidence 0.7). Core macros are internally consistent and plausible. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database as published in a peer-reviewed journal (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care, 2008). Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, mineral breakdown, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese chicken fried rice dish are not available as a discrete entry in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (ID 35188), HPB Singapore Foodo, or FAO/INFOODS SEA as of 2026-04-17. trans_fat_g is 0.02 g/100g (trace level from cooking oil); this is a confirmed low value consistent with USDA SR Legacy composite data for stir-fried rice dishes. Input source was GrabFood (ai_generated, confidence 0.7); values were retained after cross-check against USDA SR Legacy approved-source composite. Halal status is marked false as oyster sauce (shellfish-derived) is a standard ingredient; halal-certified variants may exist but cannot be confirmed without vendor verification. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database fried rice (white rice base) entry as published in Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2008; no direct measurement for Vietnamese chicken fried rice was found.
